findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.suggestion: You need a dedicated slide that shows how your product actually works and why it's better than what exists today. For example, if you're in logistics, show a side-by-side comparison: "Traditional approach = manual dispatch taking 4 hours, our platform = AI-matched routes in 15 minutes," then briefly explain the core tech or process that makes that possible. This is where investors see the "aha" moment of your idea.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.suggestion: You need to add a competitive landscape slide that shows where you sit relative to 2-3 direct competitors—map them on a simple 2x2 grid using axes that matter to your customers (like price vs. ease of use, or speed vs. customization). Include one sentence on why you're differentiated on each axis, so investors see you've thought through the competitive set and aren't just hoping no one else exists.

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: [S6] Zeevaa Financial Model — Revenue_Model_Projection_Final.pdf (Internal) Used for Slides D, G, H, J — SOM $22.3M by 2030, $2.8M Year 1 target, unit economics, subscription assumptions

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market size and problem articulation, but investors will want to see that real buyers actually feel this pain the way you've described it. Add a new slide after your Problem slide showing 3-4 direct quotes from buyer interviews (even 15-minute calls count) that validate the specific friction points you've called out—for example, a buyer saying "I had to manage documents across WhatsApp with three different agents and lost track of my KYC paperwork" or an agent saying "80% of my deals stall because buyers can't coordinate the handoff." Even 5-10 interviews with the right people will shift this from "market research says there's a problem" to "we

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.suggestion: You're showing massive market size numbers, but investors need to know exactly who you're going after first. Add a dedicated slide before your market data that defines your initial customer segment with specifics like "mid-market real estate agents in Dubai with 5-50 transactions/year generating AED 2-10M in annual commission" or "property developers closing 20+ units annually in UAE." Then show how many of these specific customers exist and what their pain point is worth to them — this makes your $2.8M Year 1 target credible instead of arbitrary.

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.suggestion: You have the +25% YoY growth data but it's buried in your citations—bring it to the problem slide itself as a visual callout showing transaction velocity is accelerating, making the friction you're describing increasingly costly. Add a simple line: "H1 2025: +25% YoY transaction growth means more deals, more chaos, more failures" right next to your 99% deal failure stat so investors see that the problem is getting worse, not static.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: Powered by Claude AI (Anthropic) — search, legal review, risk flagging, daily follow-up. RAG-only verified responses. Anthropic Claude API (LLM & AI Engine) . RAG pipeline · Intent parser · Context retrieval City Specific Trained SLM → School catchments · Commute isochrones POI discovery · Hyperlocal pricing. Property suitability scoring, buyer intent prediction, mortgage probability scoring, developer inventory matching. Dubai-trained Small Language Model: school catchments, commute isochrones, POI discovery, hyperlocal pricing. Intent + context-aware. Sub-200ms SLA gate mandatory before any user sees results.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: City-tuned SLM → Dubai-trained Small Language Model: school catchments, commute isochrones, POI discovery, hyperlocal pricing. Own hyperlocal map (planned)
findings.suggestion: Your Dubai-trained SLM is your strongest moat candidate, but you're burying it in a tech stack list—investors need to see why competitors can't replicate it. Add a slide that shows the specific data advantage: how many DLD transactions you've trained on, what accuracy lift you've achieved versus a generic Claude model on Dubai-specific queries (e.g., "school catchment + commute + hyperlocal pricing accuracy: 94% vs. 67% baseline"), and crucially, your exclusive data partnerships (DLD API access, DXBinteract, market data feeds) that create a widening moat as you accumulate more transaction data. This transforms a technical feature into a defens

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Production-ready stack — live pre-funding · RERA licence active · AML/KYC (PDPL) built-in from day one · Sub-200ms search SLA mandatory gate

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: AI-guided advisors for mortgage decisions, legal review, site visits, and DLD transfer appointments. Real people, live data.
findings.suggestion: Right now your tech slide shows what you built, but investors need to see the buyer's actual journey—walk them through one complete transaction from "I want to buy a property" to "keys in hand," showing exactly where AI handles what and where your consultants step in. For example: "Buyer searches Dubai properties → AI surfaces 3 matches with school catchments and commute data → buyer books site visit → consultant guides in-person, flags legal risks → AI pre-approves mortgage pathway → consultant handles DLD transfer → done." This makes your PhyDigital model tangible instead of abstract.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Powered by Claude AI (Anthropic) — search, legal review, risk flagging, daily follow-up. RAG-only verified responses. Anthropic Claude API (LLM & AI Engine)

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: AML/KYC (PDPL) built-in from day one · Sub-200ms search SLA mandatory gate

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: LTV = ARPA ÷ churn rate · Blended CAC = S&M spend ÷ new paying customers · 10% annual subscription churn

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.suggestion: Add a simple line to this slide stating your current monthly burn rate and how many months of runway you have left — something like "Current burn: $180K/month | Runway: 14 months (through Q3 2025)" positioned near your revenue trajectory. Investors need to know the clock you're working against to contextualize why this funding round matters now, and it's a basic credibility check that shows you're tracking your own finances closely.

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: CAC: $2,100 (2026) → ~$800 (2028) · network effects drive 62% reduction. 16 : 1 LTV:CAC (Year 3). LTV by customer segment: Developer ~$272,500, B2C Buyer ~$35,000+, Homeowner ~$49,000, Broker/Agent ~$27,250

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: The same financial figures appear consistently across both presentations of Slide 9: Revenue $2.8M → $22.3M in 4 Years, CAC $2,100 (2026) → $800 (2028), 16:1 LTV:CAC, 74% Gross Margin 2026, 43% EBITDA 2030 target
findings.suggestion: Great news—your numbers are locked in consistently, which builds credibility. Now add one line showing the math behind your CAC collapse claim: something like "CAC drops 62% as organic referrals grow from 15% to 48% of new customers by 2028" so investors see this isn't just an assumption but a specific driver tied to your unit economics model.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated "Use of Funds" slide that breaks down exactly where this capital round is going—for example, something like "Series A allocation: 40% product engineering (expand API capabilities), 30% go-to-market (developer community programs + broker partnerships), 20% operations/team, 10% runway buffer." This gives investors confidence you've thought through priorities and helps them see how their money directly accelerates the unit economics you're already showing.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.suggestion: You're showing strong unit economics and a 43% EBITDA target by 2030, but investors need to see the exact quarter or year you hit breakeven—add a callout like "Breakeven: Q3 2027" directly on your revenue/EBITDA chart so it's impossible to miss. Right now they have to infer it from the trajectory, which leaves room for doubt about your path to cash flow positivity.

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're showing transaction volume and a revenue target, but investors need to see actual paying customers today—not future milestones. Replace "$2.8M revenue target" with something like "$340K ARR from 12 active real estate brokers in Dubai (Q4 2025)" or whatever your current paying customer base actually is, then show how you're expanding that cohort into the next phase. This proves the model works with real money, not just projections.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: One Platform. Four Markets. Sequenced for Capital Efficiency. Single configurable localisation engine — no platform rebuild per market. UAE proves the model. Every subsequent market is a deployment.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: You've got milestones and revenue targets ($2.8M, $6M, $10.7M), but investors need to see the growth trajectory between them—add the month-over-month or quarter-over-quarter percentage increases alongside those numbers. For example, instead of just listing "$2.8M revenue target" for UAE phase one and "$6M rev" for phase three, show "Q2 2026: $2.8M → Q1 2027: $6M (114% growth in 9 months)" so they can immediately see the velocity and validate your unit economics assumptions.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: Right now your roadmap shows acquisition milestones (agents, transactions, revenue targets) but investors need to see that users are *staying* — add a single retention metric to each phase, like "72 transactions / 85% agent retention" in UAE or "48 txns, $6M rev, 6-month cohort retention 78%" in the USA phase. This proves your model works beyond just signing people up, especially critical since you're expanding to four markets and need to show each one compounds rather than churns.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.suggestion: You've got real traction numbers here—72 transactions, 200 agents, $2.8M revenue target—but investors won't know if these are from actual customers or projections. Add one concrete customer win to this slide: name a real estate brokerage, developer, or fintech partner that's already live on your platform in UAE, even if it's just "Leading Dubai brokerage: 48 live transactions, $1.2M GMV to date" or similar. This transforms the roadmap from "we plan to do this" to "we're already proving it works."

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: Ariz Rizvi Founder & CEO · Zeevaa... Jaimohan Pillai CTO · Zeevaa... Sales Lead (UAE) Month 1... Mortgage / FinTech Mgr Month 2–3
findings.suggestion: You're showing Sales Lead and Mortgage Manager as future hires rather than current team members—that's a real problem for a fintech pitch because investors need to see you already have someone who understands mortgage distribution and can close deals in your target market. Bring on your Sales Lead before your next investor meeting, even if part-time, and make sure they have direct mortgage or financial services sales experience you can point to on the slide.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Ariz Rizvi: 20+ industry experience, London Business School MBA, $150M+ in completed UAE real estate transactions, Active RERA Broker Licence. Jaimohan Pillai: Ex-Lloyds, and Barclays, over 3 decades of experience in Data science, Senior industry executive with 30+ years across real estate, financial services, and enterprise technology in the UAE and wider GCC.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: Advisory board to be formed post-seed
findings.suggestion: You need to name at least 2-3 people who've already committed to advising you—even informally—before you close this round. Investors want to see that experienced people believe in your vision enough to help you now, not someday; for example, if you're building a fintech product, mention "Sarah Chen, former Head of Payments at [Bank], is advising on our go-to-market strategy" rather than leaving it blank until post-seed.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single slide after your team section that shows your personal equity stake and vesting schedule—something like "Founders: 40% fully vested, Key Hires: 20% vesting over 4 years with 1-year cliff." Investors need to see you're aligned long-term and that you've thought through how to retain talent, so this transparency actually builds confidence rather than raising questions.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: RERA Licensed Only AI platform legally authorised to collect commissions & hold escrow — a regulatory moat no tech competitor holds. RERA Broker Licence: Ariz Rizvi Real Estate LLC
findings.suggestion: You've got a genuine competitive advantage here that investors in regulated spaces care deeply about — your RERA license and escrow authority are real moats — but they're buried or missing from your deck entirely. Add a dedicated compliance slide (even just one) that shows your RERA Broker License, the specific commissions and escrow rights it grants you, and one concrete example of how this blocks competitors (like "competitors can't legally hold customer deposits, forcing them to use third-party payment processors that take 2-3% cuts").

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You don't need to add this unless you actually have pending litigation or IP disputes—investors will ask directly if something exists. If you do have any legal issues (even minor ones like a trademark dispute or a licensing question you're working through), add a single bullet on your Operations or Risk slide that names it factually and shows your mitigation plan, like "Trademark opposition filed by [Company]—resolved through [specific action] by Q2 2024."

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.suggestion: No issues found here — your numbers are internally consistent across the deck. Keep this up as you add new slides or update projections; the best time to catch inconsistencies is before they reach investors, so do a quick cross-check whenever you revise financial assumptions or historical figures.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: Only AI platform legally authorised to collect commissions & hold escrow — a regulatory moat no tech competitor holds.

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.suggestion: Since no specific formatting issues were detected, do a final pass on your deck: open it in presentation mode, read every word aloud to catch typos, and verify that font sizes, colors, and alignment match across all slides—small inconsistencies like one slide having 28pt titles and another having 32pt will make investors notice sloppiness before they hear your story.
